title: FLIP Grids, Drag Cubes, Shimmer Type, Momentum Reorder & Custom Elements
description: Five fresh UI drops: FLIP-animated filter grids, a drag-controlled 3D cube, sweeping shimmer text, spring-powered reorder, and a native Custom Element tab bar.

drops.title: FLIP-Animated Filter Grid
drops.description: Filtering or shuffling a card grid reflows every surviving card with the classic First-Last-Invert-Play technique.
drops.concept: Instead of cross-fading or jump-cutting a grid when items are filtered, sorted, or shuffled, FLIP captures each card's position before the DOM change, applies the change, then inverts the visual delta with a transform and animates it away. The result is every card visibly gliding to its new slot at 60fps using only cheap transform/opacity properties. It's the technique behind most of the smooth filterable portfolio and product grids you see today.

interaction.mouse: Click a filter chip or Shuffle
interaction.click: Filters/reorders the grid with an animated reflow

drops.title: Drag-Rotate Content Cube
drops.description: A literal 6-face CSS 3D cube you spin by hand — drag to rotate, release to snap to the nearest face.
drops.concept: Six panels are positioned as the faces of a real cube using transform-style: preserve-3d and per-face rotateX/rotateY + translateZ offsets. Pointer drag maps directly to rotateY (and a clamped rotateX for tilt), and on release the drag velocity feeds a momentum calculation that lands the cube on the nearest 90° face. It reframes a familiar 'rotating gallery' idea as something the visitor physically controls instead of watching scroll past.
drops.source: Codrops 3D cube gallery tutorial (tympanus.net) — rebuilt as a drag-controlled vanilla cube instead of scroll-driven

interaction.mouse: Drag to rotate, release to snap
interaction.click: Toggle auto-rotate in the debug panel

drops.title: Shimmer Sweep Gradient Text
drops.description: A moving highlight band clipped to text via background-clip: text — looping, hover-triggered, and scroll-triggered variants.
drops.concept: A linear-gradient with a bright band is clipped to the text shape (background-clip: text, color: transparent) and animated across it by shifting background-position. It's one of the most common 'premium SaaS headline' effects on the web right now, and it's almost entirely free performance-wise since it runs on the compositor. This drop shows the same recipe firing three different ways: an ambient loop, a one-shot hover sweep, and a scroll-triggered reveal via IntersectionObserver.

interaction.click: Replay button on the third headline
interaction.touch: Scroll to trigger the third headline

drops.title: Momentum Drag-to-Reorder List
drops.description: Drag a row and its neighbors shift live; release fast and a real spring simulation overshoots into place.
drops.concept: Every row is transform-positioned, so dragging one just updates its translateY while a live index check swaps its slot with whichever neighbor it has crossed, shifting that neighbor with a smooth transition. On release, the row's exit velocity seeds a small spring integrator (stiffness/damping loop) that lets it overshoot its final slot and settle — a tactile, physical finish that a plain CSS transition can't produce.
drops.source: Taha Shashtari's drag-to-reorder tutorial (tahazsh.com) — extended with velocity-based spring settle

interaction.mouse: Drag a row up or down

drops.title: Native Custom Element Tab Bar
drops.description: A real <spot-tabs> element built with customElements.define — sliding indicator, full keyboard nav, no framework.
drops.concept: Rather than a CSS-only or React tab component, this is an actual browser-native Custom Element registered with customElements.define(), wiring up ARIA tab/tabpanel roles, arrow-key navigation, and a measured sliding indicator entirely inside connectedCallback(). The same <spot-tabs> tag is reused three times — horizontal, vertical, and icon-only — to show it's a genuinely reusable primitive, not a one-off snippet.

interaction.touch: Tap tabs; arrow keys also navigate
